title: Nepal FM's Delhi Visit Institutionalizes Criminal Justice Cooperation with MLAA Entry
lede: Nepal's Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal visited India on June 5-6, 2026, to advance bilateral ties across development, connectivity, and trade while clearing internal processes for the India-Nepal Mutual Legal Assistance Agreement in Criminal Matters to enter into force.

sections.context: Foreign Minister Khanal's official visit to New Delhi, conducted at the invitation of India's External Affairs Minister Dr. S. Jaishankar, marks a consolidation of institutional mechanisms in the India-Nepal partnership. The bilateral talks on June 6, 2026, covered the full spectrum of relations—development cooperation, connectivity, trade and transit, energy, and people-to-people ties—reflecting the breadth of engagement between the two countries. Most significantly, both ministers welcomed the completion of internal processes for entry into force of the India-Nepal Mutual Legal Assistance Agreement in Criminal Matters (MLAA), a legal instrument that creates formal institutional channels for criminal justice cooperation. This timing suggests a deliberate effort to operationalize governance frameworks that have been under negotiation, signaling readiness to deepen institutional ties beyond the diplomatic level.
sections.threads: This visit aligns with a broader pattern of India-Nepal bilateral deepening documented in recent engagements. The ministers expressed satisfaction with progress across 'diverse sectors' and welcomed new initiatives in innovation and startups, digital and financial technology, and training and capacity building—vectors that indicate [[india-np-india-and-nepal-expand-bilateral-agenda-beyond-energy-i-2026|a diversification of the bilateral agenda beyond traditional energy cooperation]]. The MLAA's entry into force complements this expansion by establishing legal scaffolding for cross-border criminal investigations and judicial cooperation. Energy cooperation—historically the centerpiece of India-Nepal relations—remains part of the reviewed agenda but now sits alongside newer domains. The emphasis on people-to-people ties and capacity building suggests an effort to address governance and institutional quality, areas that have historically been friction points in bilateral relations.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the practical operationalization of the MLAA once it enters into force. The agreement's effectiveness in handling cross-border criminal cases—particularly those involving financial crime, organized networks, or fugitive extradition—will be a key metric of whether institutional deepening translates into tangible outcomes. The ministers' commitment to 'intensify efforts towards further enhancing the multifaceted India-Nepal partnership' warrants attention to follow-up mechanisms: whether joint working groups are convened, implementation timelines are set, and resource allocation occurs. The innovation and fintech cooperation pillar is notably new to the public record and deserves scrutiny regarding [[india-np-indias-financial-corridor-reaches-nepal-upi-launch-signals-n-2024|whether it extends existing financial integration initiatives]]. Over the next 6-18 months, expect announcements on the MLAA's first operational applications and clarity on the scope and funding of the startup and digital cooperation tracks. Any shifts in energy negotiation intensity or new connectivity projects would also signal whether this institutional expansion is sustainable or performative.
